item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There isn't much to this one. It has a decent hook in the beginning but goes nowhere interesting. Also I should note; police WILL respond to calls no matter how ridiculous they are. Emergency operators are required to assume NOTHING is a prank call. You could try it for yourself (please don't actually. I'm just making a point.) If you pick up the phone right now and report a massive spasming asshole opened up in the ground in front of you, I promise you they will do something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll admit that yes, I did miss your statement about preferring it as an odd, inexplicable event (your other critique caught my attention enough that I missed the rest), so allow me to clarify:

I don't agree with your feedback because it applies to a direction for the article I don't intend to take.

My goal was to write an event that, while on surface level, seems to make correlative sense, but, underneath, is still as odd as it was before. If I wrote it to lack any rhyme or reason I would've been unsatisfied with the final product. And, regarding your interpretation of my reply, you missed the point that the mystery over the sound is meant to be so minor that maintaining it would detract from the core of the article, and that it'd be pointless with regards to my intent.

The comment about lung damage was not in response to the part on importance; it was to the part on moralizing (and the implication that "moralizing" holds as a term in implying a hamfisted message). The lung damage is important to the article; the sounds, on the other hand, serve to maintain an intrigue to keep the article moving and allow for a sense of vague understanding before the final line.

Also, if you think 3707 is inexplicable with nothing else, then I'd highly recommend reading it over again and paying attention to the themes it tries to evoke.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't see why there'd be a need to mystify the sound, considering that the primary focus of the article isn't on a deep mystery.

Nearly 1/5th of the story is devoted to this mystery, and it is the main driving force of the story. It’s one of the primary questions of the article along with “What even is this thing?” And it’s clearly important enough to warrant a place in the ending paragraph. Therefore, one could imagine that it deserves something better than one line explaining exactly what was going on.

It’d be like if an adventure novel had a subplot wherein two characters are in a relationship. If in the ending there was a line which stated that “Character X and Y married and lived happily ever after”, I would also be infuriated. Yes, it’s not the main focus of the story, but it holds enough weight to warrant a better explanation.

Not everything needs to be "shown" in a story if it isn't crucial. "Show don't tell" was also a genuine CIA psyop, but that's besides the point.

Right, I agree that unimportant things should be struck. But this mystery is a crucial element of the story. If it wasn’t, then there wouldn’t be any inclusions of it at all in the story, let alone a place in the ending.

+ show block
– hide block

I also agree with you that the CIA using “show don’t tell” to silence writers is not relevant to this conversation nor this story.

Lung damage from construction is. A common thing? And not abnormal enough to be hamfisted in this context.

The fact that it’s being included at all means that it’s important to the story. Like you said, something shouldn’t be shown in a story if it isn’t crucial. And since it’s the ending line and placed immediately after a sentence which explains what the mysterious sounds were, it appears to be an explanation for what this anomaly is: Walter’s payback to the construction site for causing him to develop silicosis.

If that feels like "moralizing" to you, and that's something you have issue with in the first place (stories have always been used to communicate messages) then that's not something I'm interested in changing.

You miss the point by miles. And the next sentence too, which explains what I meant: “It's fine as is, but I would've preferred it simply being an incredibly odd event with no rhyme or reason for occurring when it did.”

I’m not saying that it’s bad for stories to have morals or an obvious message to the readers. I’m saying that this article doesn’t need one. There are plenty of articles (SCP-4710 and SCP-3707 come to mind) which just describe weird events with no message, no moral, and no explanation. It leaves the reader with an unanswered question, which makes the article stick in their mind.

I believe that not having a moral would give this article that quality, and would make everything else that much more weird.


As a courtesy to our readers on mobile devices, please collapse long posts. ~Zyn

While the imagery is nice and the "spasming asshole" joke is both funny and a great hook, I didn't feel particularly interested in what was being presented. I think, narratively speaking, it needs a bit more kick to it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The ending could've been done a lot better considering that it straight up tells the reader what the sounds represent.

I didn't see why there'd be a need to mystify the sound, considering that the primary focus of the article isn't on a deep mystery.

Showing that could've drawn me into the story just enough to where everything else clicked.

Not everything needs to be "shown" in a story if it isn't crucial. "Show don't tell" was also a genuine CIA psyop, but that's besides the point.

I'm also not a fan of the last line, since it puts a moral to this whole thing.

Lung damage from construction is. A common thing? And not abnormal enough to be hamfisted in this context. If that feels like "moralizing" to you, and that's something you have issue with in the first place (stories have always been used to communicate messages) then that's not something I'm interested in changing.

Fixed the SPAG error.

(link on the psyop changed after finding a better article on the subject)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The ending could've been done a lot better considering that it straight up tells the reader what the sounds represent. Showing that could've drawn me into the story just enough to where everything else clicked. I'm also not a fan of the last line, since it puts a moral to this whole thing. It's fine as is, but I would've preferred it simply being an incredibly odd event with no rhyme or reason for occurring when it did. The rest of the article had good imagery, though.

Downvote for now, might turn into a novote later.

EDIT: SPAG error fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
love it when a roller explodes out of me

+1
